summary: Task 17 delivers a clean Draft→Persistent atomicity model: commitDraftChat rewrites paths inside the Room transaction, Attachment.Audio correctly includes stagedFilename in equals/hashCode so StateFlow dedup cannot swallow the staging-complete update, the hard-gate in runInferencePersistent short-circuits before persisting USER, and the send-gate guards against mid-flight commits. However two concrete correctness issues remain: (1) a filename-collision race in concurrent addImages → writeAttachmentStaging for images admitted in the same batch, and (2) MVP silently drops all but the first image when the user attached >1 image in either Draft or Persistent mode — the extra images flow to inference but are never persisted, and the user gets no feedback that their picture disappeared from history. Both should be addressed before this lands.

findings.description: Filename-collision race when addImages admits multiple images in one call. ChatViewModel.addImages decodes a batch of URIs, appends them to _attachments, then iterates `admitted.forEach { stageDraftAttachmentIfNeeded(it) }` — each invocation does its own `viewModelScope.launch { ... withContext(ioDispatcher) { writeAttachmentStaging(...) } }`. These launches run concurrently. Inside writeAttachmentStaging, `nextAttachmentFilename(stagingDir, ...)` reads `dir.listFiles()?.size` to pick the next index. There is no serialization between the size-read and the file-write, so two (or more) coroutines that both observe N=0 will both pick `img_0.png`. Whichever write lands last overwrites the earlier one; both in-memory attachments end up with the same stagedFilename; on commit, commitDraftChat writes `attachments/{chatId}/img_0.png` onto the first message's imagePath (only one file survives), and the second attachment's bitmap is silently lost on disk even though the UI still shows both thumbnails until commit. The test suite does not cover this because ChatViewModelTest only ever admits one image at a time via addImageBitmap, and the FakeChatRepository generates monotonic indices from a VM-side counter (stagedImageIndex++), masking the repo-level race entirely. The matching tech-spec note (Details → Edge cases, last bullet about `System.nanoTime()` / mutex) acknowledged the concern and left it unresolved.
findings.recommendation: Pick one of: (a) serialize writes per-stagingDir with a `Mutex` inside DefaultChatRepository (cheap — staging is short); (b) swap filename generation to `System.nanoTime()` or a UUID suffix so collisions are statistically impossible (the spec already suggested this in `Edge cases`); or (c) replace `list().size` with an atomic counter per stagingDir held on the repository (`ConcurrentHashMap<File, AtomicInteger>`). Option (a) is the smallest change — wrap `nextAttachmentFilename` + `writeAttachmentPayload` in a per-`stagingDir` Mutex. Also add a ChatViewModelTest where addImages receives ≥2 URIs and asserts that the two admitted Image attachments end up with DIFFERENT stagedFilename values; only a real DefaultChatRepository (not the fake) exercises the race.

findings.location: app/src/main/kotlin/app/sanctum/machina/ui/chat/ChatViewModel.kt:684-689 (commitDraft: firstNotNullOfOrNull picks the first staged image/audio) and ChatViewModel.kt:765-781 (runInferencePersistent: persistedImage = pending.firstOrNull { it is Attachment.Image })
findings.description: MVP behaviour silently discards additional images. When the user attaches 2+ images to a single USER turn (allowed by MAX_IMAGES=10), the VM persists only the first image path on the MessageEntity row (both in the Draft commit path via `stagedImage = firstNotNullOfOrNull { ... }` and in the Persistent path via `persistedImage = pending.firstOrNull { it is Image }`). The remaining images ARE forwarded to the inference engine (images = pending.filterIsInstance<Image>().map { it.bitmap }), so the model reasons about them — but they never land on disk and therefore vanish from chat history after the turn completes. There is no snackbar, no log, no UI affordance hinting that only one image is kept. A user attaching 5 photos to a single message will see all 5 as input thumbnails, receive an answer that references all 5, then scroll back in history and see only 1 attachment on the USER bubble. This is the exact shape of silent data loss Phase 3 was meant to eliminate (see task 17 rationale: `Для пользователя это потеря данных`). Note the Draft staging ALSO writes only one staged image filename to disk (the VM's `stagedImageFilename` param is single-valued, matching MessageEntity's single `image_path`), so the other staged files in .staging-{uuid}/ are orphaned after rename — they end up under `attachments/{chatId}/img_1.png` etc. but nothing references them, and startup sweep doesn't touch files under committed chat dirs. Accumulating orphan bytes per send.
findings.recommendation: Two options: (i) enforce MVP at the input layer — clamp `MAX_IMAGES` to 1 while MessageEntity carries a single image_path, so the issue surfaces as an input-layer snackbar (`attachment_max_images_reached` already exists), matching MessageBubble's single-image render shape; or (ii) document the limitation in the USER bubble (e.g. `+N more` badge) and add an errorLog.w when `pending.count { it is Image } > 1` so orphan writes are at least diagnostic. Option (i) is the cleanest for MVP — it prevents the staged-but-orphaned writes in Draft AND aligns input UX with persistence capacity. Before picking, raise with the user: the task file's Implementation notes explicitly accept 'one image per message row' — the remaining question is whether MAX_IMAGES>1 is still desired for inference-only or should be reduced.

findings.description: attachmentsRootFor derives filesDir from `stagingDir.parentFile?.parentFile` and only checks that the immediate parent is named `attachments`. Combined with the containment check below, this is correct for the well-formed case (`filesDir/attachments/.staging-uuid`), but breaks when stagingDir has a trailing slash or a non-canonical path: `parentFile` operates on the raw path component, not the canonical form. For example, `File('/data/app/files/attachments/')` → `parentFile.name == 'files'`, not 'attachments' — writeAttachmentStaging would reject what is in fact a legitimate attachments root caller if a caller ever passed the root itself. More importantly, the containment check uses `candidate.canonicalPath.startsWith(attachmentsRoot + File.separator)` — this means `attachments` itself is not allowed, only children are allowed — which is fine for staging, but the error surface is ambiguous when a caller mis-nests (e.g. `filesDir/attachments/subdir/.staging-uuid`, `parent.name == 'subdir'`, rejected by `attachmentsRootFor` with 'must live directly under attachments/'). There is also no test for the `parent.name != ATTACHMENTS_DIR` branch. This is not a security break (the canonicalPath check still guards the write), but the two-layer containment logic is harder to reason about than the original commitDraftChat single-check pattern; future callers could easily forget the 'directly under attachments/' invariant.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) take filesDir explicitly as a parameter on writeAttachmentStaging (same shape as savePersistentAttachment) to avoid the parent-derivation dance, OR (b) add a test that exercises the 'staging nested two levels under attachments/' rejection path and the 'trailing slash on stagingDir' case. (a) is the more maintainable option and aligns the two new methods' signatures.

findings.description: The onFailure branch does `viewModelScope.launch { errorLog.e(...) }` while itself already running inside a `viewModelScope.launch { ... }.fold(...)`. The inner launch is not wrong (errorLog.e suspends on IO and the outer coroutine is free to return), but it makes the stack a bit confusing. The same pattern is repeated at ChatViewModel.kt:820-827 and at 786-792 for runInferencePersistent — the justifying comment at :818 notes 'errorLog.e suspends on Dispatchers.IO' and the author wants the UI to unblock immediately. That is fair, but since we are already inside a launched coroutine the extra launch only saves the duration of the IO write (typically negligible). Consider extracting a small helper `fun logAsync(component: String, msg: String, cause: Throwable? = null) { viewModelScope.launch { errorLog.e(component, msg, cause) } }` and invoking it inline — removes the nested-launch noise and documents the intent.
findings.recommendation: Add a private helper on the VM for detached error logging and replace the nested launches at 501-507, 419-421, 786-792, 820-827. Cosmetic — not blocking.

findings.location: app/src/main/kotlin/app/sanctum/machina/ui/chat/Attachment.kt:36-40 (Image is a plain `class`, not a data class, and does not override equals/hashCode)
findings.description: Attachment.Image is a regular class so equals/hashCode fall back to reference equality. The stagedFilename update in stageDraftAttachmentIfNeeded.onSuccess creates a NEW Image instance with the same bitmap and id — a different reference, so StateFlow's distinctUntilChanged (on ==) fires and emits the updated list. This works by accident. Attachment.Audio was given an explicit equals/hashCode override that INCLUDES stagedFilename (the comment on the class explains why). If Image ever gains an equals override in the future (e.g. to compare by bitmap identity / id), forgetting to include stagedFilename will silently drop the staging-complete emission — the exact bug the Audio override was added to prevent. Make the invariant explicit to avoid a future regression.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) add a matching equals/hashCode override on Image that includes stagedFilename (parallels Audio), OR (b) add a short KDoc note on Image stating 'relies on reference equality for StateFlow updates; do NOT override equals without including stagedFilename'. (b) is lower-effort but (a) is more robust.

findings.description: The late-race comment in task 17's description claims 'we null draftStagingDir on onSuccess before any late remove lands'. That holds for the commit success path. But there is another race: user attaches → staging write coroutine begins → user taps × BEFORE the write returns. `removeAttachment` runs, calls `deleteStagedFileIfAny(removed)`, but `removed.stagedFilename` is still null (write hasn't completed), so deleteStagedFileIfAny returns at line 519 (the `?: return` on filename). Then the staging coroutine lands, writes the file, and updates _attachments.map — but the attachment is no longer in the list, so map() is a no-op. Result: file on disk, no in-memory reference. The startup sweep cleans .staging-* dirs on next cold start, so it is self-healing — but within the same process lifetime the file lingers. Acceptable per the task's edge-case note ('Remove во время concurrent write в staging … принять как edge-case'), so this is documentation-only: the invariant `deleteStagedFileIfAny after removal always deletes the file` does NOT hold, and the code currently relies on startup sweep for full cleanup. Worth a KDoc on deleteStagedFileIfAny so a future reader does not mistake this for a guarantee.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-line KDoc on deleteStagedFileIfAny: 'If the staging write is still in flight when the user removes the attachment, the eventual write will produce a file that only the startup sweep reclaims.' Optionally track staging-in-flight Jobs by attachment.id in a Map and cancel on remove; the task accepted the simpler behaviour so this is optional.

findings.description: addAudio guards against a second audio at lines 450-451 (early return if alreadyHasAudio), then `_attachments.update { current → if (current.any { it is Audio }) current else current + audio }` re-checks the same invariant (defensive against a race with a concurrent mutation), then lines 457-459 verify the audio actually landed by id before calling stageDraftAttachmentIfNeeded. This is three checks for one invariant, and the addImage* siblings do not use the same defensive pattern (addImageBitmap just `_attachments.update { it + image }`). Not wrong, but inconsistent and a bit bloated. Either addImageBitmap should adopt the same defensive update (there IS a similar race for images when the user taps at MAX_IMAGES boundary), or addAudio should trust the initial read (the audio invariant is a single-writer invariant from the UI).
findings.recommendation: Harmonise the two paths. Prefer either (a) always trust the eager read (drop the update-block `any` checks in addAudio), or (b) use the update-block race guard in both. (a) is simpler given VM access is MainThread.

findings.description: The catch clause catches Throwable, deletes the partial file, and re-throws. Catching Throwable also swallows CancellationException through the `throw t` — but the `target.delete()` line runs first. If the coroutine was cancelled mid-write, the delete may itself race with further cleanup, and the CancellationException propagation is preserved. This is acceptable, but the cancellation-safe idiom is to rethrow CancellationException immediately before the cleanup: `catch (ce: CancellationException) { target.delete(); throw ce } catch (t: Throwable) { target.delete(); throw t }`, or at minimum use `CancellationException` in the catch list and rethrow quickly. Matches the pattern used elsewhere in the codebase (e.g. StartupHousekeeper:87-88 explicitly rethrows CancellationException).
findings.recommendation: Split the catch into `catch (ce: CancellationException) { throw ce }` (or run delete on a NonCancellable scope if we want the cleanup to survive cancellation) + `catch (t: Throwable) { target.delete(); throw t }`. Keeps cancellation semantics aligned with the rest of the Phase-3 code.

findings.description: In Persistent mode the VM calls savePersistentAttachment twice in sequence (once for image, once for audio). If the image write succeeds but the audio write throws, the code falls into the hard-gate (correct — inference does not start, USER not persisted), but the image file the first call wrote under `attachments/{chatId}/img_0.png` remains on disk with no referencing row. Next `savePersistentAttachment` call in the same chat will see `list().size == 1` and use `img_1.png` — so subsequent writes don't collide, but the `img_0.png` file is an orphan until the chat itself is deleted (deleteChat removes the whole dir). The task's Details → Edge cases note 'Partial-fail тут не критичен: chatId стабилен, sweep on startup подметёт orphan файлы' — however the startup sweep only handles .staging-* dirs and zombie chats with no messages. Orphan files under committed chat dirs are NOT swept. So this is strictly `accumulates bytes on failure until chat delete`, which is a real leak for users who hit ENOSPC often — but in practice rare.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) delete the image file if the audio write fails (best-effort cleanup on the failure path), (b) accept the leak and document on savePersistentAttachment's KDoc that 'partial-fail leaks bytes until the chat is deleted', or (c) extend the startup sweep to reclaim files in committed chat dirs that no MessageEntity references (more invasive — Task 6 scope). (a) is a 3-line change on the failure path and keeps the invariant clean.

findings.description: `import java.io.File` sits between `import app.sanctum...` and `import dagger...`. The rest of this codebase (e.g. DefaultChatRepository.kt) groups java.* imports after the app.* / kotlinx.* groups. Not load-bearing, but a ktlint / style-check will grumble.
findings.recommendation: Move `import java.io.File` and `import java.util.UUID` to the java group (usually after kotlinx). Cosmetic.

positives: commitDraftChat correctly rewrites imagePath/audioPath inside the same Room @Transaction as the INSERTs and the rename — the row + disk agree at commit time, which was the central AC-A6 invariant.
Attachment.Audio.equals/hashCode deliberately include stagedFilename with a load-bearing KDoc — this is the kind of invariant-documenting comment that prevents silent regressions.
stageDraftAttachmentIfNeeded.onSuccess maps by attachment.id, so a late success cannot re-insert an attachment the user removed — the race described in the review brief is not a bug.

runInferencePersistent hard-gate on savePersistentAttachment failure correctly returns BEFORE savePersistentMessage(USER), restores attachments for retry, resets isGenerating, and emits attachment_save_failed. The Ready state is restored cleanly.
draftStagingDir = null on commit success happens BEFORE any late deleteStagedFileIfAny can target the final chat dir — the ordering inside the onSuccess block is correct.

Test coverage for the new behaviours is solid: send-gate-while-staging-in-flight uses a CompletableDeferred to deterministically exercise the race; hard-gate tests on both USER-persist and attachment-persist failure paths; commitCalls verification that stagedImageFilename round-trips into commitDraftChat.
The ChatRepository interface documentation on the two new methods is exemplary — containment guarantee, partial-fail behaviour, and caller responsibilities are all called out.

Test fakes (FakeChatRepository) were updated cleanly with stagingWrites / persistentAttachmentCalls counters so assertions are specific; the eventLog optional cross-fake ordering is a nice reusable primitive for AC-R1-style invariants.
